text: when I had nowhere else to go, and nothing else to do. This was my prolonged suicide attempt, since I didn’t have the courage to swallow a bullet the right way. Every now and then a young woman will stumble my way, attracted to the moth in the corner, something about my dark presence appealing to her sense of adventure. The smart ones will come closer, and then take a step back, unsettled by something they see—the glint in my eye, perhaps, the wave of negative energy that surrounds me, maybe, or just the general sense of suffering and emptiness in my manner of dress, messy hair, and uneven beard. Those that are deeper into their drink, less afraid, or seeking something empty to complement their own void, sometimes sit down next to me, chatting me up, putting a hand on my muscled, sweaty forearm, leaning in to get a better look. The last time one went home with me she got up after we were done with our exertions, casting a wary eye my way. She picked up her clothes off the floor and started to get dressed. “I have to go,” she said. “There’s something in here with us, and I don’t like it.” I would have argued with her if she was wrong. “You should get some help, before it eats you alive. Before it finishes the job.” I take a deep breath and exhale. “I was hoping that might be you,” I said. Her fit body glistened in the dim light, as she slowly covered herself back up, putting as many layers of cotton and wool between us as she could. She ran her hands through her long, red hair, untangling it, and then put it up in a ponytail. She couldn’t help glancing at me, looking up as she slid on her socks, peeking over her shoulder as she pulled on her jeans and gray sweatshirt. Keeping an eye on me. Afraid to look away for too long. “This is way beyond me. Get help. And don’t call me.” She crossed to my side of the bed and sat down, trembling, placing a hand on my bare chest. “You’re not a bad man,” she said. “But you have to let this go. It’s toxic. And the waves it gives off are palpable.” She kissed me on the forehead, shuddered, and then left. In the bar, I sit and watch, preferring to stay as far away from the customers as I can. And yet, I hate to be alone. I hate to be alone with my thoughts. To remember. To understand my role in the way things played out. There is no end to the pain that I can see, it goes all the way to my core, and on that bent and swaying framework I hang the last of my withering hope. # In the shadows, there is giggling, a click, and the sound of a heavy door opening, and then tiny footsteps running away. Later there is a loud noise, and even later still, the closing of a door, a bath being run, and a heavy sigh, followed by gentle sobbing. # It’s on a slow night, Wednesday, I think, when the tall, thin man slides into the booth across from me, dressed as if he had an important meeting with a board of directors, or perhaps a body to bury. It’s early still, the long, empty tavern filled with the distant bass and muffled lyrics of some lonely crooner, or goth lizard king. The sparkling of the bottles on the wall echoes the string of sad Christmas lights that run the perimeter of the room, the tang of orange disinfectant barely covering up the sour smells of vomit, urine, and something rotting. He doesn’t say anything at first, only gives me a cursory glance as he sips a martini, his pinkie finger extended. I am currently holding my glass of bourbon, the bottle not quite out of reach, head bent over, gazing into the swirling liquid—always looking for answers. And never finding them. For a moment I wonder if he’s even here, his presence too quiet and still. And then his eyes turn to me, two deep pools of obsidian, and it’s my turn to shiver. His pale skin is almost translucent, blue veins running up his neck, and down the back of his hands. The suit is dark, some shade of black that holds a slight glimmer to it, his dress shirt blindingly white, but no tie. The skin of his neck, it’s a different shade of white, almost a blue tint to it, and as I stare at the indentation above his collar bone, I feel like he might be hairless, like a newly born rat. The way he carries himself as though he is muscled and sinuous, at rest, but ready at a moment’s notice to unleash the violence within. I am both attracted to him, and repulsed at the same time, eager to touch his skin, knowing it must be ice cold, but fearful that it might give way, and reveal something squirming underneath. I shake my head and catch his gaze. “Something I can do for you?” I ask. “Maybe,” he says. He reaches into his suit coat, and pulls out a thin, brown, leather wallet. Though it may not be leather at all. Something about it is off, but I try not to stare. If I didn’t know any better I’d say a few stray hairs stuck out of it, but he opens it and pulls out a business card before I can honestly judge what he carries. He hands the white card to me and I take it. “And maybe I can do something for you in return.” The card says Jeremiah Fisher, Proprietor. There is no name of the business, just an address over on the west side of town, Cicero Avenue. And no phone number or email, just a glyph of three inverted crosses,
